title: What to do when your Wise account is under review

author.name: Samuel Chen
author.bio: A former product manager at a major fintech company, Samuel has deep expertise in the global payments landscape. He analyzes financial tools and strategies to help freelancers maximize their earnings and minimize fees.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Proactively maintain a 'compliance-ready' digital dossier with your ID, business registration, and proof of address to resolve any platform review in hours, not weeks.
Always use clear, unambiguous payment references tied to specific invoice numbers to avoid triggering automated anti-money laundering (AML) flags.
Structure your financial flows logically by letting large client payments 'settle' for a few days before distribution to avoid mimicking high-risk transaction patterns.
Eliminate single-point-of-failure risk by building a diversified 'financial stack' with separate platforms for client payments, operational spending, and a pre-vetted contingency channel.
summary: When your financial account is frozen, the immediate problem is a complete halt to your business cash flow, causing operational paralysis. The core advice is to shift from panic to methodical action by compiling a "proof of legitimacy" dossier to expedite the review while professionally managing client communications. Ultimately, by adopting this process and diversifying your finances into a resilient "stack" of multiple platforms, you resolve the crisis and build a stronger business that is no longer vulnerable to a single point of failure.
ai_overview_answer: When your Wise account is under review, the first step is to methodically compile a "Proof of Legitimacy" dossier and communicate professionally with their support team. Immediately begin documenting the situation by taking screenshots of all notifications and saving every email from Wise. Proactively gather essential documents into a single folder, including your government ID, proof of address, business registration, and the specific contracts or invoices related to recent large transactions. Before contacting Wise, inform any affected clients of a "temporary processing delay" to manage expectations. To prevent future reviews, always use clear payment references tied to specific invoice numbers and ensure your transaction patterns are logical. For long-term resilience, build a "financial stack" by using multiple platforms for different purposes and establishing a pre-vetted contingency payment channel. This ensures that a freeze on any single account does not halt your entire business operation.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

itemListElement.name: What to do when your Wise account is under review

@graph.name: What to do when your Wise account is under review
@graph.description: Facing a Wise account under review? Learn how to manage the crisis, compile the right documents, and prevent future freezes with our expert 3-phase plan.

step.name: Phase 1: Triage & Control — Your First 24-Hour Action Plan
step.text: Execute a crisis communications protocol by notifying clients of a temporary delay. Become a forensic archivist by screenshotting all notifications and logging all actions. Compile a 'Proof of Legitimacy' dossier with your ID, proof of address, business documents, and recent transaction proof. Communicate with Wise support concisely, attaching your dossier to expedite the process.

step.name: Phase 2: Prevention & Hardening — How to Make Your Account Bulletproof
step.text: Establish a permanent 'Compliance-Ready' digital folder with all your core business documents. Master 'clean' transaction narratives by using clear, professional invoice references and avoiding high-risk keywords. Structure your transfers logically, allowing large payments to settle before moving them to avoid triggering AML flags.

step.name: Phase 3: Resilience & Diversification — Why a Single Platform Is the Disease
step.text: Adopt a 'financial stack' mentality by using different platforms for specific roles (e.g., Wise for client payments, Revolut for spending, local bank for taxes). Segregate funds into dedicated sub-accounts for revenue, taxes, and salary. Identify and prime a contingency payment channel (like PayPal or Stripe) to ensure business continuity during a freeze.

mainEntity.name: How do I prevent my Wise account from being put under review?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Prevention is a matter of discipline, not luck. It’s about operating a transparently legitimate business.
1.  **Proactively Verify:** Complete every level of identity and business verification available within the platform. Don’t wait to be asked.
2.  **Use Painfully Clear References:** Every client payment must be tied to a specific invoice number (e.g., "Payment for Invoice 145B"). Vague references are a primary red flag.
3.  **Maintain Logical Patterns:** Ensure your transaction patterns make sense. Avoid moving large sums in and out rapidly or receiving payments from entities unrelated to your documented business activities.
4.  **Keep Documents Ready:** Maintain your "compliance-ready" folder with updated contracts and corporate documents so you can respond to any query instantly.

mainEntity.name: What documents are needed for a Wise 'source of funds' review?

acceptedAnswer.text:
When regulators ask "where did the money come from?" you must provide a clear, documented answer. This typically includes:
*   **Signed Client Contracts or Service Agreements:** The foundational document proving a legitimate business relationship.
*   **Corresponding Invoices:** Each invoice should match a payment amount and reference a specific contract.
*   **Business Bank Statements:** These should show incoming payments from clients that correspond to the invoices.
*   **Source of Wealth Declaration:** If you funded the account with a large sum from personal savings, property sales, or investments, be ready to document that origin with asset sale agreements or personal bank statements.

mainEntity.name: Can I still receive money if my Wise account is under review?

acceptedAnswer.text: In almost all cases, no. When an account is under review, its core functionalities—sending, spending, and receiving—are typically suspended. This is a crucial security measure for the institution. Any incoming payments sent to your account during this period will likely be held and then returned to the sender. This reality underscores why a pre-vetted contingency payment channel is an absolute necessity.

mainEntity.name: Wise account review vs. account closure: What's the difference?

acceptedAnswer.text: The distinction is critical. A **review** is a temporary inquiry. The platform has flagged an issue and is pausing activity to gather information. Your objective is **resolution**: provide clear documentation to get the account reinstated. A **closure**, however, is a permanent decision by the institution to terminate the relationship. Your objective shifts to **recovery**: the orderly and complete withdrawal of any remaining funds to your other bank accounts.

mainEntity.name: What are the most common triggers for a Wise account review?

acceptedAnswer.text:
While some reviews are random, specific events often trigger automated flags. Understanding them enhances your operational awareness. Common triggers include:
*   Receiving an unusually large payment that deviates from your account's history.
*   Transactions involving high-risk jurisdictions or entities on international watchlists.
*   Mismatched information, such as the name on an incoming payment not matching your verified account name.
*   Using ambiguous or suspicious payment references.
*   Logging in from multiple, geographically disparate locations in a short period.
*   Providing documents that cannot be easily verified, such as an address on a bill that doesn't match your registered business address.

body.content: Phase 1: Triage & Control — Your First 24-Hour Action Plan

When your Wise account is under review, the first 24 hours are critical. The goal is to shift from helpless anxiety to empowered action. You are not a passive observer awaiting a verdict; you are the CEO of your business. It's time to methodically build your case while managing the operational fallout. This is about taking back control.

items: **Execute a "Crisis Comms" Protocol:** Before contacting Wise, manage your most important asset: client trust. An unexpected account freeze can jeopardize payments, but professional communication solidifies relationships. Immediately notify any clients whose payments might be affected. Do not overshare or convey panic. Frame it with professional poise: *"We are experiencing a temporary processing delay with a third-party payment partner and are working to resolve it. We will provide a direct update on your payment within 48 hours. We appreciate your patience."* This brief, honest communication preserves goodwill and buys you invaluable breathing room.

**Become a Forensic Archivist, Not a Frantic User:** Your most powerful tool is documentation. Emotional, rambling emails to customer support are counterproductive. Instead, adopt the mindset of a calm, meticulous archivist. Create a dedicated folder labeled "Wise Compliance Review - [Date]."
    *   Screenshot every notification, banner, and alert inside your Wise account.
    *   Save every email from Wise as a PDF, naming each file chronologically.
    *   Log every action you take with a timestamp (e.g., *10:15 AM: Uploaded passport copy. 10:17 AM: Submitted support ticket #123456.*).
You are building a clear, unemotional case file. This record is your leverage and proof of cooperation.

**Compile Your "Proof of Legitimacy" Dossier:** At its core, a compliance review is about verifying that you and your business are legitimate and compliant with Anti-Money Laundering (AML) regulations. Anticipate their requests. Proactively gather digital copies of the documents they will almost certainly ask for into a single, clearly-labeled folder. This demonstrates professionalism and dramatically expedites the process. Your dossier should include:
    *   **Identity:** A crystal-clear scan of your government-issued ID (passport or driver's license).
    *   **Address:** A recent utility bill or bank statement clearly showing your name and address.
    *   **Business Legitimacy:** Your business registration documents, articles of incorporation, or official trade licenses.
    *   **Proof of Transactions:** For recent large transactions, gather the corresponding client contract, the exact invoice, and potentially a bank statement from your client showing the funds leaving their account.

**Communicate with Precision:** Once your dossier is ready, contact support. Your first message sets the tone. Be concise, factual, and professional. Reference a specific transaction ID if the review seems tied to one. Attach your pre-compiled dossier.

    *"Subject: Urgent: Account Under Review - [Your Name/Business Name]*

    *Dear Wise Support Team,*

    *My account has been placed under a compliance review. To help resolve this as quickly as possible, I have proactively attached a dossier containing my proof of ID, address, business registration, and the contract/invoice for my most recent large transaction. Please let me know what other specific information you require. Your prompt attention is greatly appreciated."*

This approach transforms you from a panicked user into a professional partner in the security process, making it easier for the compliance officer to clear your account.

body.content: Phase 2: Prevention & Hardening — How to Make Your Account Bulletproof

The methodical, evidence-based approach you just used is not a one-time fix; it is the foundation for a stronger business. A compliance review is a painful but powerful stress test that reveals operational weaknesses. Now, we will deliberately harden your financial operations to prevent this from recurring. Consider this your inoculation against future compliance shocks.

items:
**Establish a "Compliance-Ready" Digital Folder:** Think of this as your business's permanent due diligence file. Create a secure, cloud-synced folder that is always up to date. The goal is to respond to any compliance request in minutes, not days. This folder should contain evergreen copies of:
    *   Your core business registration or LLC formation documents.
    *   A template for a "Source of Wealth" or "Source of Funds" declaration—a simple, signed letter explaining that your income is derived from professional services.
    *   Your primary client Master Service Agreements (MSAs) or standard contract templates.
    *   Quarterly, add your updated proof of address (a recent utility bill will suffice).

**Master "Clean" Transaction Narratives:** This is one of the most critical and overlooked aspects of maintaining good standing. Fintech platforms use automated systems to scan transaction descriptions for keywords that could indicate high-risk activity. Always use clear, professional, and unambiguous payment references.

rows: My fee for the Havana project

body.content: Never use jokes, slang, or words that could trigger automated sanction flags. This includes the names of comprehensively sanctioned countries or regions (e.g., Iran, North Korea, Syria, Cuba, Crimea) even in an innocent context. An algorithm sees the keyword, not the context, and this can be an immediate trigger for an account freeze.

items:
**Structure Your Transfers Like a CFO:** Erratic financial behavior is a major red flag for AML systems. Your transactions should tell a logical story.
    *   **Align Payments with Invoices:** If you send a client an invoice for $15,000, the incoming payment should be for $15,000. Avoid receiving multiple, small, un-invoiced payments from a single client in a short period.
    *   **Avoid Rapid In-and-Out Movements:** Moving large sums into your account and then immediately transferring them out to multiple other accounts is a classic money laundering pattern. When you receive a large client payment, let it settle. Pay contractors, move your salary, and set aside taxes in separate, deliberate transactions over a few days, not all within the same hour. This demonstrates purposeful, legitimate business activity.

body.content: As fintech regulations evolve, you cannot predict how a platform’s rules will change. You can, however, control the quality and organization of your own documentation. Your goal is to be so prepared that these external shifts cause minimal disruption. A surprise request for documents should be a minor administrative task, not a business-halting crisis. By hardening your operational habits, you are fundamentally upgrading the professionalism of your Business-of-One and building the infrastructure to support more complex clients and larger payments.

This internal discipline is what allows you to absorb external shocks. But true resilience demands more. The account review has revealed an uncomfortable truth: your business has a single point of failure. A genuine CEO does not tolerate this kind of structural risk. It’s time to stop thinking like a freelancer who simply *uses* a payment tool and start acting like a CFO who strategically *architects* a financial system.
Phase 3: Resilience & Diversification — Why a Single Platform Is the Disease

The ultimate goal is to make any single platform—Wise included—a component of your strategy, not its foundation. This requires adopting a "financial stack" mentality, layering different services to create a robust and flexible system. Never again will one compliance review on a single platform have the power to halt your entire operation.

items: **Embrace the "Financial Stack" Mentality:** Your business should not live in one institutional house. A resilient financial stack recognizes that different tools have different strengths. Think in terms of specialized roles.

rows: Optimized for low-cost international invoicing and currency conversion.
Excellent for day-to-day business expenses, debit card spending, and managing currencies on the go.
Anchors your business in your country of residence for paying taxes and building a local credit history.
A pre-vetted backup (e.g., PayPal, Stripe) to redirect funds during a primary account freeze.

items: **Build a Moat with Segregated Accounts:** Once you have your stack, create firewalls *within* it. Most modern financial tools offer sub-accounts, often called "Pots," "Spaces," or "Vaults." Use them religiously to segregate funds the moment they arrive. Create three non-negotiable buckets: one for incoming client revenue, a second where you *immediately* transfer 25-30% for tax obligations, and a third for your personal salary. This discipline prevents the accidental co-mingling of funds (a major red flag for auditors) and makes your finances instantly auditable.

**Identify and Prime Your "Contingency Payment Channel":** What is your Plan B? If a client needs to pay you tomorrow and your primary account is locked, what information do you send them? This cannot be a question you answer in a panic. Have an alternative payment channel fully set up, verified, and ready to deploy. This could be a PayPal Business account, a Stripe payment link, or the wire transfer details for your local bank. Save the instructions in a template, ready to be sent to a client within minutes. The goal is to ensure cash flow is never interrupted for more than an hour.

**Reframe Compliance as a Competitive Advantage:** By architecting this resilient, multi-layered system, you are doing more than just mitigating risk. You are building a fundamentally more professional and trustworthy business. This superior level of financial organization becomes a selling point. You can onboard sophisticated corporate clients with confidence, manage global finances with reduced stress, and make bold strategic decisions from a position of absolute financial clarity. You are no longer just a freelancer hoping your payment tools work; you are the CEO of a global business with a treasury function built to last.

body.content: Building a resilient financial architecture shifts you from reacting to crises to commanding your operations. Still, tactical questions will arise. Here are direct answers to the most common concerns.

items:
**How do I prevent my Wise account from being put under review?**
    Prevention is a matter of discipline, not luck. It’s about operating a transparently legitimate business.
    1.  **Proactively Verify:** Complete every level of identity and business verification available within the platform. Don’t wait to be asked.
    2.  **Use Painfully Clear References:** Every client payment must be tied to a specific invoice number (e.g., "Payment for Invoice 145B"). Vague references are a primary red flag.
    3.  **Maintain Logical Patterns:** Ensure your transaction patterns make sense. Avoid moving large sums in and out rapidly or receiving payments from entities unrelated to your documented business activities.
    4.  **Keep Documents Ready:** Maintain your "compliance-ready" folder with updated contracts and corporate documents so you can respond to any query instantly.

**What documents are needed for a Wise 'source of funds' review?**
    When regulators ask "where did the money come from?" you must provide a clear, documented answer. This typically includes:
    *   **Signed Client Contracts or Service Agreements:** The foundational document proving a legitimate business relationship.
    *   **Corresponding Invoices:** Each invoice should match a payment amount and reference a specific contract.
    *   **Business Bank Statements:** These should show incoming payments from clients that correspond to the invoices.
    *   **Source of Wealth Declaration:** If you funded the account with a large sum from personal savings, property sales, or investments, be ready to document that origin with asset sale agreements or personal bank statements.

**Can I still receive money if my Wise account is under review?**
    In almost all cases, no. When an account is under review, its core functionalities—sending, spending, and receiving—are typically suspended. This is a crucial security measure for the institution. Any incoming payments sent to your account during this period will likely be held and then returned to the sender. This reality underscores why a pre-vetted contingency payment channel is an absolute necessity.

**Wise account review vs. account closure: What's the difference?**
    The distinction is critical. A **review** is a temporary inquiry. The platform has flagged an issue and is pausing activity to gather information. Your objective is **resolution**: provide clear documentation to get the account reinstated. A **closure**, however, is a permanent decision by the institution to terminate the relationship. Your objective shifts to **recovery**: the orderly and complete withdrawal of any remaining funds to your other bank accounts.

**What are the most common triggers for a Wise account review?**
    While some reviews are random, specific events often trigger automated flags. Understanding them enhances your operational awareness. Common triggers include:
    *   Receiving an unusually large payment that deviates from your account's history.
    *   Transactions involving high-risk jurisdictions or entities on international watchlists.
    *   Mismatched information, such as the name on an incoming payment not matching your verified account name.
    *   Using ambiguous or suspicious payment references.
    *   Logging in from multiple, geographically disparate locations in a short period.
    *   Providing documents that cannot be easily verified, such as an address on a bill that doesn't match your registered business address.

body.content: From Compliance Anxiety to Financial Command

The shock of a frozen account is a powerful lesson. While the immediate goal is resolving the review, the ultimate opportunity is to fundamentally upgrade your operational resilience. A compliance review is not just a bureaucratic hurdle; it is a stress test revealing the precise points of failure in your financial infrastructure. By implementing the framework of Triage, Prevention, and Diversification, you transform from a reactive freelancer into the strategic CEO of an antifragile Business-of-One.
This is a conscious mindset shift away from dependency and toward command.

header: Reactive Freelancer Mindset (The Path to Anxiety)
Strategic CEO Mindset (The Path to Control)
rows: Panic. Fire off emotional emails to support. Wait helplessly while cash flow halts.
**Execute.** Immediately inform clients of a processor delay, compile a "Proof of Legitimacy" dossier, and log every action.
Operate with messy finances. Use vague payment references and move money erratically.
**Harden.** Maintain a "compliance-ready" digital folder, use crystal-clear invoice references, and structure funds transfers logically.
Rely entirely on a single fintech account for all business. A single freeze means total paralysis.
**Architect.** Build a resilient "financial stack"—multiple providers for different purposes—and maintain a pre-vetted contingency channel.

body.content: Adopting this CEO mindset means you internalize the hard-won lesson of an account review: never again will a single point of failure have the power to halt your business. **Triage** becomes a practiced drill, not a panicked scramble. **Prevention** becomes a system of professional habits that builds trust with your financial partners. Most importantly, **Diversification** becomes your ultimate strategy for financial resilience. You turn a moment of crisis into a permanent state of control.
